pick.rationale: UConn is the higher seed (2 vs 7) with a 29-5 record and elite defensive efficiency, while UCLA's analytical ratings (KenPom 27, Torvik 24) are strong but not at UConn's level. The Huskies' tournament experience under Dan Hurley and their ability to control tempo should be decisive, though UCLA's offensive firepower and recent form make this a competitive matchup. UConn's overall talent depth and defensive prowess give them the edge in this Round of 32 contest.

reasoningStep.title: UCLA vs UConn Round of 32 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: UConn favored as 2-seed with superior defensive metrics and tournament pedigree, but UCLA presents a credible upset threat with strong offensive efficiency.
evidence: UConn holds a 29-5 record as a 2-seed with elite defensive efficiency, while UCLA is 23-11 as a 7-seed with KenPom rank 27 and Torvik rank 24.
UConn's tournament experience under Dan Hurley and their defensive prowess provide crucial advantages in high-pressure March games.
UCLA's offensive efficiency (rank 17-22) could challenge UConn, but the Huskies' overall talent depth and ability to control tempo should prevail.
Multiple sources indicate UConn is on upset alert, suggesting UCLA has a credible path to victory, but the higher seed's defensive strength and experience make them the safer pick.

(2) Seed Preview Andrew Mintner Mar 16, 2026 3 1 Share Welcome back!! In this article, I will do a historical analysis of the (2) seeds in this tournament. If this is your first time reading my work, I suggest you go back and read some of the previous March Madness articles to get acclimated to the terminology and certain angles I will be discussing. Now, my entire March Madness analysis is based on a database I have built using KenPom statistics dating back to 2002. The key when doing this is that I am comparing the previous (2) seeds with each other. For example, if I say a (2) seed ranks 26th in AdjEM, that means they rank 26th out of the other 92 (2) seeds in the KenPom era. As I have stated in previous articles, when I identify a team with red flags, that doesn’t mean the team cannot succeed; it means they are highly unlikely to overperform, based on other teams with the same red flags. The reason I use certain red flags is that they have led to underachievement relative to historical advancement rates for certain seeds. I hope that makes sense. If not, feel free to reach out to me, and I will explain the best I can. Let’s dive in!!! Thanks for reading The Mintner Method! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work. Subscribe Glossary: I use specific KenPom data, and his entire glossary is available at his website. I use several formulas that I created, which I will explain throughout my articles. If you do not have a KenPom subscription, I highly recommend signing up. There is no better source of advanced statistics for College Basketball. As the saying goes, you cannot win a bracket contest in the first weekend, but you can lose it. These previews are specifically designed to weed out pretenders and identify value in teams. The vast majority of the public overestimates their ability to identify contenders. To make that easier for you, let me introduce you to some red flags.
